name: italian asian stuffed bell peppers
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS. Both cook the bell peppers SEPARATELY from the filling, and both finish with off-heat basil (or basil + parsley) and shredded monterrey jack (or another soft cheese). Gochujang is folded into the filling for a background savory-spicy warmth that pushes the dish into italian-asian territory (see notes).

VARIANT A — MEAT (OR VEGAN MEAT):
- Sauté finely diced yellow onion and celery in olive oil until softened — this is the savory base.
- Add ground beef (or impossible/vegan ground meat); cook until done, breaking it up.
- Stir in herbes de provence, black pepper, and a spoon of gochujang. Salt to taste.
- Off heat, fold in torn fresh basil leaves and chopped fresh parsley (see herb note).
- Spoon into the (separately-cooked) bell pepper halves. Top with shredded monterrey jack and broil briefly to melt.

VARIANT B — SEASONED TOFU CRUMBLES (PREFERRED):
- Crumble super firm tofu by hand into rough small chunks.
- Toss the crumbles with potato or corn starch, a heavy pinch of salt, garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, and black pepper.
- Bake at ~425°F until the crumbles are browned and crisp on the outside.
- In a bowl, combine the baked tofu crumbles with FINELY DICED RAW celery (not sautéed — see notes), torn fresh basil leaves, chopped fresh parsley, herbes de provence, and a spoon of gochujang stirred through.
- Spoon into the (separately-cooked) bell pepper halves. Top with shredded monterrey jack and broil briefly to melt.
notes:
TWO VARIANTS, ONE SHARED FRAME: both versions use bell peppers as the boat, cheese as the dairy finish, fresh off-heat basil (with parsley) as the aromatic. The filling protein and the celery treatment are what differ. Gochujang runs through both variants.

=== ITALIAN-ASIAN — WHY GOCHUJANG BELONGS ===

This recipe used to be plain italian stuffed peppers. It's now italian-asian: gochujang is folded into the filling as a background savory-spicy note that pairs cleanly with the softened onion + celery + meat (or tofu crumble) base. It doesn't overpower the basil or the herbes de provence — it sits underneath them as a fermented-chili depth that gives the dish more dimension than the plain italian version had. A spoonful is enough; you're not making the dish spicy, you're adding depth. This is the load-bearing new ingredient — the whole rename is about it.

=== HERBS — BASIL + PARSLEY, BASIL ALONE IF THAT'S ALL YOU HAVE ===

BASIL + PARSLEY IS THE PREFERRED HERB COMBINATION: torn fresh basil leaves stay non-negotiable as the italian aromatic finish; chopped fresh parsley alongside it is a real upgrade. Parsley adds a green herbaceous background that lets the basil sit forward without carrying the whole herb load alone. Both go in off heat at the same moment.

IF YOU ONLY HAVE ONE HERB, USE BASIL: parsley is the addition, not the substitute. Basil-alone is still the fallback that reads correctly; parsley-alone doesn't. If you have both, use both.

=== SHARED TECHNIQUE ===

BELL PEPPERS COOKED SEPARATELY — DON'T BAKE BASIL INTO THE PEPPER: cook the bell pepper halves on their own (oven or skillet), then add the prepared filling at the end. Baking the basil into the pepper kills the basil — same logic as basil-in-pasta-sauce: it goes off heat at the end, never cooked in.

BASIL OFF HEAT, ALWAYS: torn fresh basil leaves go in last, off heat. This is the single most load-bearing technique note across both variants. Cooked basil reads stale and the dish loses its italian register. Parsley same treatment — off heat with the basil.

MONTERREY JACK (OR ANY SOFT CHEESE): monterrey jack is the canonical pick — melts cleanly, doesn't compete with the basil. Other soft melty cheeses (mozzarella, fontina, soft provolone) work fine. Hard cheeses (parmesan, aged cheddar) don't — they don't melt right for this format.

=== VARIANT B IS PREFERRED ===

VARIANT B IS BETTER: I prefer the tofu-and-raw-celery variant to the meat one, for two reasons:
- Tofu is cheaper than meat, and the seasoned-and-baked crumbles carry the savory load just as well.
- The raw finely-diced celery makes the texture less monotonous. In variant A, the celery is sautéed into the base alongside the onion and meat — everything ends up the same softened texture. In variant B, the celery stays crunchy and contrasts with the crispy-baked tofu crumbles. Each forkful has two textures (crisp tofu + crunchy celery) instead of one (uniform softened filling).

SEASONED TOFU CRUMBLES — SUPER FIRM, STARCH-COATED, HEAVILY SEASONED, BAKED CRISP: the variant B technique is its own thing worth getting right.
- Use SUPER FIRM tofu, not firm. Super firm holds its shape during the crumble-and-bake without needing to be pressed.
- Crumble by hand into rough small chunks — not pulverized, you want bite-sized pieces.
- Toss with potato starch or corn starch — coats the surface, gives you the crispy-fried texture during the bake.
- HEAVY salt and seasonings — tofu is bland baseline; under-season it and the dish reads flat. Garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, salt, black pepper all on a generous hand.
- Bake hot (~425°F) on a sheet pan until crisp and browned. The starch coating is what crisps.

VARIANT A — SAUTÉED ONION + CELERY + MEAT IS THE SAVORY BACKBONE: in the meat variant, the trio of softened yellow onion, softened celery, and cooked ground meat (real or faux) is the structural filling. The cheese on top adds dairy depth; the basil and parsley add the aromatic finish. The mistake here would be skimping on the onion or undercooking it — both make the filling read flat.

CELERY — DIFFERENT TREATMENT BY VARIANT: variant A wants celery softened into the savory base; variant B wants celery raw and finely diced for textural contrast. Same vegetable, different role. Don't sauté the celery for variant B and don't leave it raw for variant A — each variant's choice is load-bearing for that variant's texture goal.

HERBES DE PROVENCE: works in both variants. Goes into the cook (variant A) or tossed in with the assembly (variant B). It's a background herb register, not a finishing herb — distinct from the off-heat basil/parsley job.

SIBLING RECIPES:
- italian-asian-tofu-plate: the deconstructed, no-skillet, self-serve version of this same flavor family. Baked tofu + basil/parsley salad + raw bell pepper + cream cheese glob + gochujang-vinegar dipping sauce + lime wedges. Reach for it when you want protein density without the assembly work.
